title: Take a Seat
passage:
For years the benches on Fältström Square were bolted to the paving and faced the traffic, and for years they sat mostly empty. When the district finally swapped them for a scatter of loose, lightweight chairs that anyone could drag into the shade or up to a neighbour's table, the planners braced for theft. Almost none went missing. What they had not predicted, notes the urban designer Tobias Wren, who logged the square across two summers, was how the plain fact of being movable changed the way people used it: a chair turned a few degrees to catch a conversation did more for the square's sociability than any of the costlier fountains and planters installed the same year. Wren is wary of drawing a rule from one square. "A plaza is not a formula," he writes, "and what loosens one crowd may scatter another." Even so, he leaves the reader with a quiet suspicion that a city's warmth may rest on things light enough to lift.
— Marion Delacroix, city desk

questions.prompt: What does the passage suggest about what makes a public square sociable?

options.text: That the loose chairs were rarely stolen, contrary to what the planners had feared before the swap.
That small, movable features people can rearrange themselves may matter more than costlier fixed installations.
That cities ought to stop spending on fountains and planters in their public squares altogether.
That replacing fixed benches with movable seating will reliably bring life to any empty plaza.

questions.rationale: TYPE-002 inference, one logical inch beyond the text. The passage reports that a movable chair 'did more for the square's sociability than any of the costlier fountains and planters', and closes on the suspicion that warmth 'may rest on things light enough to lift'; B generalises that observation cautiously (note the hedge 'may matter more'), which is the intended inference. A = too_literal: the theft detail is a true restatement from the passage, but it answers a different question (what happened to the chairs, not what makes a square sociable) and requires no inference. C = two_step_leap/outside_knowledge: the writer says the chairs did more, never that cities should stop funding fountains — and he explicitly resists drawing prescriptions ('wary of drawing a rule'). D = quantifier_upgrade/scope_error: 'reliably... any empty plaza' absolutises a hedged, single-square observation and is directly refuted by Wren's caution that 'a plaza is not a formula, and what loosens one crowd may scatter another'.

generator_meta.topic: urban design: movable lightweight seating vs fixed benches and costly features, and its effect on plaza sociability

fictional_source.byline: Marion Delacroix, invented city-desk columnist; observation attributed to invented urban designer Tobias Wren
fictional_source.publication_type: weekend city supplement of a general newspaper (BrE house style)

generator_meta.spelling_note: BrE held throughout ('neighbour'); no AmE-only spellings present.
generator_meta.stem_law_note: Stem asks what the passage SUGGESTS about square sociability; it does not name or entail the empirical outcome. The reported outcome (movable chairs outdid costlier fixed features) is an empirical finding the passage could have reported either way — the planners had in fact predicted the opposite (theft, low use).
